content.full_text:
Jack E. Gordon                                    September 1, 1976
Manager, Collections-Admin.
& Exempts Section
Franchise Tax Board


                                                           A-96 54C

Delbert L. Spurlock, Chief
Conflicts of Interest Division

Mr. Gordon's memorandum of August 13 -
"Meaning of Foreseeably"


Thank you for your instructive memorandum. Your comments therein
reinforce the Conflicts of Interest Division view that recent
Commission actions regarding clarification of requirements of
conflict of interest codes and alterations of regulations in 2
California Administrative Code Section 18700 et seq. will have
little or no effect upon the staff's previous recommendation for
approval of the Conflict of Interest Code of the Franchise Tax
Board.

The Conflicts of Interest Division is preparing a package of
materials which outline all the Commissions recent actions regard-
ing the development of conflict of interest codes. That complete
package will be transmitted to the Franchise Tax Board on September
15.

Enclosed are portions of that package for the purpose of enabling
the Board to undertake a systematic review of its proposed Code in
light of the Commission's new posture. The proposed Code of the
Board will be heard at the Commission's meeting of September 21
and 22, 1976. I will be in touch with you for the purpose of dis-
cussing any proposed changes which you may wish to institute in
light of the Commission's determinations. A copy of your memoran-
dum will be made a part of the record of review of the Code for
the Franchise Tax Board.


DLS:jhm

Enc.


bcc : JP

State of California                                          Franchise Tax Board

Memorandum                                                    F P P C
                                                             [illegible] 28 PM 76    August 13, 1976

To    :  Delbert Spurlock, Chief
         Conflicts of Interest Division
         Fair Political Practices Commission             File No.:  410:JEG:ah
         1100 Kay Street
         Sacramento, CA    95814


From  :  Jack E. Gordon


Subject:  Meaning of "Foreseeably"


         The past several meetings of the Commission have led me to believe
         that it has some reservations as to the propriety of including
         the Franchise Tax Board's Auditor I's and II's within the classi-
         fication of designated employees under the Political Reform Act of
         1974.

         I wish to acknowledge at the outset that the Commission's staff
         obviously did not share its concern since, by memorandum dated
         June 30, 1976, the staff found no areas of dispute in the depart-
         ment's proposed conflict of interest code.

         I have reviewed the "Proposed Amendment" to 2, CAL. Admin. Code
         Section 18700 and submit that under this new version the depart-
         ment's auditor I's and II's must be designated under the criteria
         of participating in the making of a governmental decision.

         The proposed amendment provides in part:

              (c)  A public official or designated employee
         "participates in the making of a governmental decision"
         when, acting within the authority of his or her office
         position without--significant-substantive-review, he or
         she: ...

              (2)  Advises or makes recommendations to the
         decision-maker, either directly or without significant
         intervening substantive review, by:

Delbert Spurlock, Chief
August 13, 1976
Page 2


(A) Conducting research or investigations
requires which require(s) the exercise of judgment
on the part of the official or designated employee
and the purpose of which is to which-may influence
the decision, or

(B) Preparing or presenting any report,
analysis, or opinion, orally or in writing, which
requires the exercise of judgment on the part of
the official or designated employee and the purpose
of which is to which-may influence the decision.

The mechanics of the audit of tax returns are as follows:

1. Over the years certain criteria have been developed for the
selection of returns for audit.

2. Returns thus selected are segregated on the basis of size,
complexity and type (individual, partnership, corporation,
fiduciary, etc.) and are assigned to auditors on a random
basis.

3. The auditor then "works" the returns. Within very broad
guidelines, he decides what action is appropriate. For
example, he decides if the return deserves any action at
all. He decides the areas to be explored. He notices
what questions to ask and what verification to obtain.
He decides the amount of additional tax (or refund) due.

4. Upon conclusion of the auditor's efforts with respect to
any return, the auditor prepares a report which contains
his recommendation.

5. The auditor's report goes to the reviewer (presumably the
"decision-maker" mentioned in the proposed amendment). The
reviewer checks the report for adequacy of scope of the
audit, technical accuracy and obvious errors and, if he
approves, forwards the report for billing or refund action.

Delbert Spurlock, Chief
August 13, 1976
Page 3


Even this capsule summary of the audit process makes it clear
that the auditor participates in the making of a governmental
decision, as that activity is defined in Cal. Admin. Code
Section 18700(c)(2). No other interpretation is reasonable.

There does remain, however, the question of foreseeability. I
have read the comment on this subject attached to your memorandum of August 9, 1976. While I cannot disagree with the comment,
I find it of little practical assistance. I suggest that foreseeability may be viewed in either of two ways as it relates to the
department within the context of a conflict of interest code.

If by "foreseeable" the Commission is only considering statistical probability, i.e., mere chance that an auditor will be
assigned a return where by his action he could materially affect
his financial interest, then probably few, if any, of our
employees should be designated. We work with such a substantial
percentage of the population that the odds are simply too great
to consider it foreseeable that any one auditor will be assigned
a return which, by his action thereon, could affect his financial
interests.

If, however, "foreseeable" refers to the possibility that any
designated position or group of designated positions might be
staffed by a person or persons who may improperly use his position
to favorably affect his financial interest, then I suggest our
auditors must be designated. Among the powers of the auditors is
the authority to generally request any return he wishes and, once
a return is in his possession, he may recommend virtually any
action he wishes. He may, for example, obtain the return of a
relative in whose business he has invested and he may "no change"
that return. There is every likelihood that such action would
go unchallenged.

If, however, such an employee is designated and his business
interest is a matter of public record, it is submitted that he
is much less likely to make the improper move. And even if he
does, designation and disclosure provides a check on such actions.

Delbert Spurlock, Chief
August 13, 1976
Page 4


I submit the genesis of the Political Reform Act was the improper actions of elected and appointed officials and that the opportunity for their improprieties came not by mere chance but by overt action. I suggest that while the vast majority of people are honest and not prone to improper activity, a few are not. I further suggest the presence of those few is foreseeable. Since there is no way to single out those persons who would act improperly, an inclusive designation and disclosure policy seems appropriate.

I will appreciate your comments.

[signature]
Manager, Collections-Admin. &
Exempts Section

August ]-3. 1976

sections.question_synthetic: Whether Franchise Tax Board auditors should be designated as employees under conflict of interest code requirements, particularly regarding the interpretation of 'foreseeably' in relation to potential conflicts of interest.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC's Conflicts of Interest Division supports the staff's previous recommendation for approval of the Franchise Tax Board's conflict of interest code, noting that recent Commission actions and regulatory clarifications will have little effect on this recommendation. The Division is preparing materials outlining recent Commission actions on conflict of interest codes for the Board's systematic review before the September 21-22, 1976 hearing.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: This document contains two pieces: (1) FPPC's response memorandum from Spurlock dated September 1, 1976, and (2) the incoming request memorandum from Jack Gordon dated August 13, 1976. The FPPC response is brief and does not directly answer the substantive legal questions posed. The document discusses conflict of interest code designations and the 'foreseeability' standard. OCR quality appears moderate with some scanning artifacts visible.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Franchise Tax Board auditors should be designated as employees under conflict of interest code requirements, particularly regarding the interpretation of 'foreseeably' in relation to potential conflicts of interest.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC's Conflicts of Interest Division supports the staff's previous recommendation for approval of the Franchise Tax Board's conflict of interest code, noting that recent Commission actions and regulatory clarifications will have little effect on this recommendation. The Division is preparing materials outlining recent Commission actions on conflict of interest codes for the Board's systematic review before the September 21-22, 1976 hearing.

embedding.summary: FPPC Conflicts of Interest Division Chief Delbert Spurlock responds to Jack Gordon's August 13, 1976 memorandum regarding the meaning of 'foreseeably' in conflict of interest code designations for Franchise Tax Board auditors. Spurlock indicates the Division's support for the Board's proposed code and transmits materials reflecting the Commission's recent actions on conflict of interest code development.
